## 4.1 Block Diagram — Unified Recursive Structure of All Three Filters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Previous Belief bel xt-1] --> B[Prediction Step]
    C[Control Input ut] --> B
    B --> D[Predicted Belief bar-bel xt]
    E[Sensor Measurement zt] --> F[Measurement Likelihood pztxt]
    D --> G[Element-wise Multiplication]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Normalization by eta]
    H --> I[Current Belief bel xt]
    I --> J{Filter Type Selector}
    J --> K[Bayesian Filter: closed-form or generic integral]
    J --> L[Kalman Filter: Gaussian closed form via Kalman Gain Kt]
    J --> M[Particle Filter: weighted samples with resampling]
    L --> N[Output: mu t and Sigma t]
    M --> O[Output: set of M particles with weights]
    K --> P[Output: full belief distribution]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Kalman Filter Detailed Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize mu0 and Sigma0] --> B[Read control ut]
    B --> C[Prediction: mu-hat t = A mu t-1 + B ut]
    C --> D[Prediction: Sigma-hat t = A Sigma t-1 A transpose + R]
    D --> E[Read measurement zt]
    E --> F[Compute innovation nu t = zt - C mu-hat t]
    F --> G[Compute Kalman Gain Kt = Sigma-hat t C transpose S inverse]
    G --> H[Update mean: mu t = mu-hat t + Kt nu t]
    H --> I[Update covariance: Sigma t = I - Kt C times Sigma-hat t]
    I --> J[Return mu t and Sigma t]
    J --> K[Loop: t = t + 1]
    K --> B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Particle Filter Detailed Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize M particles from prior p x0] --> B[Set all weights w i = 1 / M]
    B --> C[For t = 1 to T]
    C --> D[For each particle i from 1 to M]
    D --> E[Sample x-tilde i from motion model p xt given ut and x t-1 i]
    E --> F[Compute importance weight w-tilde i = p zt given x-tilde i]
    F --> G{More particles?}
    G -->|Yes| D
    G -->|No| H[Normalize weights: w i = w-tilde i divided by sum j w-tilde j]
    H --> I[Compute effective sample size N-eff]
    I --> J{N-eff less than M over 2?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Resample M particles with replacement proportional to w i]
    J -->|No| L[Skip resampling]
    K --> M[Reset all weights to 1 / M]
    L --> N[Compute state estimate: x-hat t = sum i w i x i]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Return x-hat t and particle set]
    O --> C
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual Definition) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define the Bayesian filter. State its significance in probabilistic robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Bayesian filter is a recursive algorithm that estimates the posterior probability distribution $bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ over the robot state $x_t$, given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ and control inputs $u_{1:t}$. It uses Bayes' rule and the law of total probability in a two-step process: **prediction** (using the motion model) followed by **correction** (using the measurement model). Its significance lies in providing a **mathematically optimal unifying framework** for state estimation, with the Kalman Filter and Particle Filter being specific implementations under different distributional assumptions.

### Question 2 (Comparison) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between the Kalman Filter and the Particle Filter in three key aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kalman Filter | Particle Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Distribution assumption** | Assumes Gaussian distributions for prior, motion, and measurement noise | Assumes no specific distribution; represents belief via samples |
| **Handling of non-linearity** | Requires linear models; non-linear extensions (EKF, UKF) approximate the non-linearity | Naturally handles arbitrary non-linear models |
| **Multi-modal support** | Cannot represent multi-modal beliefs (single Gaussian) | Can represent arbitrary multi-modal beliefs |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Kalman Filter Focus `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) Derive the recursive Bayesian filter equation from first principles, clearly stating the Markov assumption. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Define the posterior belief:
$$bel(x_t) = p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

Step 2: Apply Bayes' rule:
$$bel(x_t) = \frac{p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}{p(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})}$$

Step 3: Recognize the normalizer:
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

Step 4: Apply the **Markov assumption on measurement** (state is complete, so $z_t$ depends only on $x_t$):
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(z_t \mid x_t)$$

Step 5: Apply the law of total probability over the previous state:
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

Step 6: Apply the **Markov assumption on state transition**:
$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$

Step 7: Recognize the prior belief:
$$p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) = bel(x_{t-1})$$

Step 8: Combine to get the **Bayesian filter recursion**:
$$\boxed{bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}}$$

**(b) For a linear-Gaussian system $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ and $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$, derive the Kalman Filter update equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: **Identify parameters.** From $x_t = x_{t-1} + u_t + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$:
- $A = 1$, $B = 1$, $R$ is the process noise covariance.

From $z_t = x_t + \delta_t$ with $\delta_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$:
- $C = 1$, $Q$ is the measurement noise covariance.

Step 2: **Prediction — Mean:**
$$\hat{\mu}_t = A \mu_{t-1} + B u_t = \mu_{t-1} + u_t$$

Step 3: **Prediction — Covariance:**
$$\hat{\Sigma}_t = A \Sigma_{t-1} A^T + R = \Sigma_{t-1} + R$$

Step 4: **Innovation** (residual between measurement and predicted measurement):
$$\nu_t = z_t - C \hat{\mu}_t = z_t - \hat{\mu}_t$$

Step 5: **Innovation Covariance:**
$$S_t = C \hat{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q = \hat{\Sigma}_t + Q$$

Step 6: **Kalman Gain:**
$$K_t = \hat{\Sigma}_t C^T S_t^{-1} = \frac{\hat{\Sigma}_t}{\hat{\Sigma}_t + Q}$$

Step 7: **Posterior Mean Update:**
$$\mu_t = \hat{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - \hat{\mu}_t) = \hat{\mu}_t + \frac{\hat{\Sigma}_t}{\hat{\Sigma}_t + Q} (z_t - \hat{\mu}_t)$$

Step 8: **Posterior Covariance Update:**
$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C) \hat{\Sigma}_t = (1 - K_t) \hat{\Sigma}_t = \frac{\hat{\Sigma}_t \cdot Q}{\hat{\Sigma}_t + Q}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose 1–2 marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to **square the noise standard deviations** (using $R = \sigma_R^2$, not $\sigma_R$).
> 2. Confusing the **prior covariance** $\hat{\Sigma}_t$ with the **posterior covariance** $\Sigma_t$ — they are different quantities at the same time step.
> 3. Dropping the normalizer $\eta$ in the Bayesian derivation. The $\eta$ must be explicitly written.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Particle Filter Focus `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain the importance sampling principle used in the Particle Filter. Derive the weight update equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: The Sampling Problem.**
The target distribution $p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$ is typically too complex to sample from directly. Importance sampling solves this by drawing samples from a simpler **proposal distribution** $\pi(x_t)$ and correcting via weights.

**Step 2: Definition of Importance Weight.**
For a sample $x^{[i]}$ drawn from $\pi(x_t)$, the importance weight is:
$$w_t^{[i]} = \frac{p(x_t^{[i]} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})}{\pi(x_t^{[i]})}$$

The weighted empirical distribution $\sum_i w_t^{[i]} \delta(x_t - x_t^{[i]})$ approximates the true posterior.

**Step 3: Expand the numerator using Bayes' theorem:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \, p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t})$$

**Step 4: Use the motion model as the proposal $\pi(x_t) = p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ and substitute the prior represented by particles at $t-1$:**
$$p(x_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t}) = \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, p(x_{t-1} \mid z_{1:t-1}, u_{1:t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$

**Step 5: After sampling, the weight simplifies to:**
$$\boxed{w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot w_{t-1}^{[i]}}$$

**Step 6: With resampling at every step, the prior weights are reset to $1/M$, so the recursion becomes simply:**
$$w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$$

followed by normalization.

**(b) Describe a programming implementation of a 2D Particle Filter for robot localization in a known map. List the key functions, their inputs, outputs, and the overall algorithm flow. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Functions in a Typical 2D Particle Filter Implementation:**

**1. `initialize_particles(M, map_bounds)`** — Input: number of particles $M$, map boundaries. Output: $M$ particles with positions uniformly sampled from the map and equal weights $w = 1/M$.

**2. `motion_update(particles, u_t, noise_params)`** — Input: previous particles, control $u_t = (v, \omega)$, process noise standard deviations. Output: new particles $x_t^{[i]} = x_{t-1}^{[i]} + u_t + \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is sampled from $\mathcal{N}(0, R)$.

**3. `measurement_model(particle, z_t, map)`** — Input: a single particle, sensor measurement $z_t = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k\}$ (range readings to landmarks), the known map. Output: the likelihood $p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$.

**4. `update_weights(particles, z_t, map)`** — Input: current particles, measurement, map. Output: updated weights $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ and normalizer.

**5. `resample(particles, weights, M)`** — Input: particles, weights, number of samples. Output: new particle set drawn with replacement proportional to weights (low-variance resampler preferred).

**6. `estimate_state(particles, weights)`** — Input: current particles and weights. Output: weighted mean pose estimate $\hat{x}_t = \sum_i w_t^{[i]} x_t^{[i]}$.

**Overall Algorithm Flow (Pseudocode):**

```python
# Pseudocode for 2D Particle Filter Localization
particles = initialize_particles(M=500, map_bounds=...)
weights = [1/M for _ in range(M)]

for t in range(T):
    u_t = read_odometry()
    z_t = read_laser_scan()
    
    # Prediction
    particles = motion_update(particles, u_t, noise_params)
    
    # Weight update
    for i in range(M):
        weights[i] = measurement_model(particles[i], z_t, map)
    
    # Normalize
    eta = sum(weights)
    weights = [w / eta for w in weights]
    
    # Resample if effective sample size is low
    n_eff = 1.0 / sum(w**2 for w in weights)
    if n_eff < M / 2:
        particles = resample(particles, weights, M)
        weights = [1/M for _ in range(M)]
    
    # Estimate state
    x_hat = estimate_state(particles, weights)
    plot(x_hat, particles)
```

**Step 7: The complete algorithm is run iteratively for each time step until convergence or task completion.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** before resampling. The weights MUST sum to 1.
> 2. **Not resampling** at all — this leads to *particle degeneracy* where one particle dominates.
> 3. **Resampling at every step unnecessarily** — this causes *sample impoverishment* and wastes computation. Use the **Effective Sample Size (ESS)** criterion: $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$. Resample only when $N_{\text{eff}} < M/2$.
> 4. Confusing the **measurement model** with the **measurement update** in code. The model computes the *expected* measurement given a state; the update multiplies the *actual* measurement likelihood into the particle weight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ✅ The Bayesian filter is the **theoretical umbrella**; Kalman and Particle filters are **practical special cases**.
- ✅ The **Markov assumption** is what makes the recursion tractable (state is complete, measurement depends only on current state).
- ✅ All three filters share the same **two-step structure**: prediction + correction.
- ✅ The Kalman Filter assumes **linear dynamics and Gaussian noise**; it is the **MMSE-optimal** estimator under these assumptions.
- ✅ The Kalman Gain $K_t$ balances trust between the **prediction** (weighted by $\hat{\Sigma}_t$) and the **measurement** (weighted by $Q_t$).
- ✅ The Particle Filter is **non-parametric** and represents belief via a **cloud of weighted samples**.
- ✅ The **Bootstrap Particle Filter** uses the motion model as the importance proposal, leading to weights proportional to the measurement likelihood.

### Critical Equations (Must Memorize)
- ✅ Bayesian recursion: $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$
- ✅ Kalman prediction: $\hat{\mu}_t = A \mu_{t-1} + B u_t$, $\hat{\Sigma}_t = A \Sigma_{t-1} A^T + R$
- ✅ Kalman correction: $K_t = \hat{\Sigma}_t C^T (C \hat{\Sigma}_t C^T + Q)^{-1}$
- ✅ Kalman update: $\mu_t = \hat{\mu}_t + K_t (z_t - C \hat{\mu}_t)$, $\Sigma_t = (I - K_t C) \hat{\Sigma}_t$
- ✅ Particle weight update: $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}) \cdot w_{t-1}^{[i]}$
- ✅ Effective sample size: $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2$

### Practical Programming Tasks (Lab-Focused)
- ✅ Implement a 1D Kalman Filter for tracking a moving object.
- ✅ Implement a 2D Particle Filter for Monte Carlo Localization (MCL).
- ✅ Plot the **true trajectory, noisy measurements, and filter estimate** to demonstrate convergence.
- ✅ Compute **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** as a performance metric.

### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Using raw standard deviations instead of **variances** in noise parameters ($R = \sigma_R^2$).
- ❌ Confusing the **predicted covariance** $\hat{\Sigma}_t$ with the **posterior covariance** $\Sigma_t$.
- ❌ Forgetting to **normalize particle weights** before resampling.
- ❌ Resampling at every step (causes sample impoverishment).
- ❌ Not resampling at all (causes particle degeneracy).
- ❌ Using the Kalman Filter on non-linear systems without using EKF or UKF.

### One-Line Exam Punchlines
- 🎯 *"The Bayesian filter is the *what*, the Kalman Filter is the *linear-Gaussian how*, the Particle Filter is the *general sampling how*."*
- 🎯 *"The Kalman Gain is the optimal weight that minimizes posterior variance."*
- 🎯 *"Importance sampling lets us sample from a complex distribution by sampling from a simple one and correcting with weights."*

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Section 5.** You are now equipped with the complete theoretical, mathematical, diagrammatic, and examination-oriented mastery of the topic *Programming Tasks to Implement Bayesian, Kalman, and Particle Filters* as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PERUT862 - Probabilistic Robotics, Module 2**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation Projects for Robotic Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT862 - PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simulation Projects for Robotic Localization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Robotic Localization Simulation** is the computational process of modelling a robot's pose estimation problem — that is, recovering the robot's position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$ in a known map — inside a software-defined virtual environment. It integrates three probabilistic components: a *motion model* $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$, a *sensor model* $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$, and a *belief representation* $bel(x_t)$ propagated via a Bayes filter or its particle-based approximation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A simulation project for localization is judged on (i) correctness of the probabilistic model, (ii) fidelity of the sensor/motion noise models, (iii) ground-truth comparison, and (iv) convergence/divergence behaviour under kidnapped-robot scenarios. ### Intuitive Analogy — The "Flight Simulator" View Imagine a commercial pilot learning to land a Boeing 737. They do **not** practise on a real aircraft at a real airport on day one. They use a **flight simulator** — a virtual cockpit that mimics turbulence, wind, instrument noise, and runway geometry. The pilot's brain continuously fuses noisy altimeter readings and visual cues to estimate the plane's position; if the estimate drifts, the simulation visually reveals the divergence and the pilot learns to correct it. A **robotics localization simulator** is the *flight simulator for the Bayes filter*: | Pilot Analogy | Robotic Localization Equivalent | |---|---| | Cockpit dashboard | Sensor suite (LiDAR, wheel odometry, camera) | | Wind & turbulence | Motion noise $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$ | | Altimeter/visual cue noise | Measurement noise $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ | | Pilot's mental position estimate | Belief $bel(x_t)$ | | Real runway | Ground-truth map $m$ | | Landing outcome | Convergence / divergence of estimate | > [!NOTE] > **Key Constants / Standard Metrics used in KTU Projects:** > - **Particle Count (M):** typically **100 – 5000** samples > - **Map Resolution:** **5 cm – 30 cm** per cell (occupancy grids) > - **Update Rate:** **10 – 40 Hz** for real-time localization > - **Convergence Threshold:** pose error **< 0.3 m** translational, **< 5°** rotational ### Why Simulate? (The Engineer's Motivation) 1. **Safety** — a wrongly localized real robot can collide with humans, walls, or expensive equipment. 2. **Repeatability** — a simulator can replay the *exact* noise sequence 1000 times, enabling statistical benchmarking. 3. **Cost** — physical sensors (LiDARs, IMUs) cost lakhs of rupees; simulation is essentially free. 4. **Ground Truth Availability** — simulators know the *true* pose, allowing pixel-accurate error measurement. 5. **Rapid Prototyping** — one can A/B test EKF vs. MCL vs. grid-Markov localization in hours, not weeks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Localization Simulation Project A KTU-grade localization project is composed of **six tightly-coupled modules**: - **A. The Virtual World (Map $m$)** Occupancy grid, landmark map, or polygon-based 2D floor plan. - **B. The Ground-Truth Simulator (Physics Engine)** A deterministic or stochastic engine that drives a "ghost" robot along a trajectory and emits raw sensor readings. - **C. The Motion Model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1})$** Typically a *sample-based odometry model* or a *velocity model* with added Gaussian noise. - **D. The Sensor Model $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$** For range finders, the **beam model** (mixture of hit, short, random, max-range components). For cameras, the **likelihood field model**. - **E. The Bayes-Filter / Particle-Filter Backend** The algorithm that fuses motion + measurement into a posterior $bel(x_t)$. - **F. The Visualization & Evaluation Layer** Renders the belief (e.g., as coloured particle cloud) and computes ATE (Absolute Trajectory Error) and RPE (Relative Pose Error) against ground truth. ### 2.2 The Bayes-Filter Foundation (Prediction → Update) Every localization algorithm is, at its heart, a recursive application of Bayes' rule: $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ - The integral $\int \ldots dx_{t-1}$ is the **prediction step** (motion projects belief forward). - The multiplication by $p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$ is the **correction / measurement update**. - $\eta$ is the normalizer ensuring $\sum_x bel(x_t) = 1$. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in KTU exam:** The *exact* form of this integral is *intractable* for continuous state spaces. Therefore, real simulators implement *approximations* — EKF (Gaussian), Grid (discrete), or MCL (sample-based). A viva question is *always* asked on this trade-off. ### 2.3 The Three Algorithmic Families | Family | Belief Representation | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---|---| | **EKF Localization** | Unimodal Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(\mu_t, \Sigma_t)$ | Computationally cheap ($\mathcal{O}(n^2)$) | Diverges under global uncertainty / multi-modal beliefs | | **Grid / Markov Localization** | Discrete histogram over grid cells | Handles global localization, multi-modal beliefs | Memory $\mathcal{O}(\vert X \vert)$, resolution-limited | | **Monte Carlo Localization (MCL)** | Set of $M$ weighted samples $\mathcal{X}_t = \{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^M$ | Approximates *any* distribution, scalable | Suffers from *particle deprivation* under kidnapping | ### 2.4 The Beam Sensor Model (Most-Used in Simulation) The probability of a range measurement $z_t^k$ for the $k$-th beam of a range-finder, given pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ and map $m$, is a four-component mixture: $$p(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_{\text{hit}} \\ \alpha_{\text{short}} \\ \alpha_{\text{max}} \\ \alpha_{\text{rand}} \end{pmatrix}^T \cdot \begin{pmatrix} p_{\text{hit}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{\text{short}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{\text{max}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \\ p_{\text{rand}}(z_t^k \mid x_t, m) \end{pmatrix}$$ with the constraint $\alpha_{\text{hit}} + \alpha_{\text{short}} + \alpha_{\text{max}} + \alpha_{\text{rand}} = 1$. - $p_{\text{hit}}$ — Gaussian centred on the true range returned by ray-casting. - $p_{\text{short}}$ — Exponential, models unexpected obstacles (people, furniture). - $p_{\text{max}}$ — Point mass at $z_{\text{max}}$ (beam missed everything). - $p_{\text{rand}}$ — Uniform $[0, z_{\text{max}}]$ (phantom / random returns). > [!NOTE] > In simulation, the ray-casting operation is implemented by the engine's native collision query (Gazebo's `RayShape`, Stage's `laser` plugin). The student is only required to compute the four mixture components on top of the engine output. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $bel(x_t)$ | Posterior belief over pose | $\eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | dimensionless PDF | | $\eta$ | Normalizer | $1 \big/ \int p(z_t \mid x', m) \, \bar{bel}(x_t) \, dx'$ | dimensionless | | $w_t^{[i]}$ | Importance weight of particle $i$ | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m)$ | dimensionless | | $\hat{x}_t$ | Best pose estimate (mean of particles) | $\frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, x_t^{[i]}$ | $(m, m, \text{rad})$ | | ATE | Absolute Trajectory Error | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \Vert \hat{x}_t - x_t^{\text{gt}} \Vert^2}$ | metres | | $N_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective particle count | $1 \big/ \sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2$ | particles | | $M_{\min}$ | Resampling threshold | $0.5 \cdot M$ | particles | | $p_{\text{odom}}$ | Odometry motion model | $\mathbb{1}[\text{within range}] \cdot \mathcal{N}(\Delta x, \Sigma_{\text{odom}})$ | PDF | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Note:** When writing the ATE formula in KTU answer sheets, the norm inside the square root **must not** be written as $\Vert \hat{x}_t - x_t^{\text{gt}} \Vert$ using a single vertical bar inside a markdown table — always extract it to a display equation block. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., Waymo, Tata ELectriCu):** simulated localization on synthetic Indian road networks before Bengaluru street deployment. - **Warehouse AMRs (e.g., GreyOrange, Addverb):** MCL-tested on digital twins of fulfilment centres. - **Agricultural Robots:** LiDAR-based localization simulated across multi-acre farms with dynamic crop rows. - **Medical Robotics:** MRI-compatible SLAM simulation in synthetic OR environments. - **Research Benchmarking:** MIT's *MIT-Campus*, Freiburg's *FR-079*, and Intel Research's *Willow Garage* datasets are all spawned from simulation engines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes Filter to Monte Carlo Localization We derive the *MCL algorithm* rigorously from first principles. #### Step 1 — Start with the Recursive Bayes Filter $$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$$ This integral is the **convolution** of the previous belief with the motion model. It has no closed form for continuous $x_t$. #### Step 2 — Replace the Continuous Belief with a Sum of Diracs We approximate $bel(x_{t-1})$ with $M$ weighted samples: $$bel(x_{t-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, \delta(x_{t-1} - x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$ Substituting: $$bel(x_t) \approx \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_{t-1}^{[i]} \, p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$$ #### Step 3 — Resample from the Old Belief If we draw a new index $j$ from the discrete distribution defined by the old weights $\{w_{t-1}^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{M}$, then $x_{t-1}^{[j]}$ is distributed *approximately* as $bel(x_{t-1})$. This is the **importance sampling** trick. #### Step 4 — Propagate Each Resampled Particle through the Motion Model Draw $\bar{x}_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$. This is the **prediction step**. #### Step 5 — Compute the Importance Weight The new weight is set to the measurement likelihood: $$w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid \bar{x}_t^{[i]}, m)$$ This is the **correction step** — particles consistent with the sensor reading get large weight, inconsistent ones get near-zero weight. #### Step 6 — Normalize $$w_t^{[i]} \leftarrow \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{M} w_t^{[j]}}$$ #### Step 7 — (Optional) Resample if Effective Particle Count is Low $$N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} (w_t^{[i]})^2} < 0.5 M \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Resample}$$ This entire derivation yields the canonical **MCL Algorithm** that KTU expects students to reproduce from memory. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Motion Model Variants | Model | Input $u_t$ | Drawback | Recommended Use | |---|---|---|---| | Velocity Model | $(v, \omega)$ | Slippage underestimation | Wheeled robots with encoders | | Odometry Model | $(\bar{x}_{t-1}, \bar{x}_t)$ | Requires accurate wheel feedback | **Most common in KTU projects** | | Map-Matched Model | $(\Delta \rho, \Delta \phi, \Delta \theta)$ from scan-match | Sensitive to dynamic objects | LiDAR-only robots | The *odometry model* is derived as follows. Given poses $x_{t-1}$ and $x_t$, the relative motion is: $$u_t = \begin{pmatrix} \hat{x}_{t-1} \\ \hat{x}_t \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{with} \quad \delta_{\text{rot1}}, \delta_{\text{trans}}, \delta_{\text{rot2}}$$ The **sampled odometry motion model** is then: $$x_t = x_{t-1} \oplus \begin{pmatrix} \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}} \\ \hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} \\ \hat{\hat{\delta}}_{\text{rot2}} \end{pmatrix}$$ where $$\begin{aligned} \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot1}} &= \delta_{\text{rot1}} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2) \\ \hat{\delta}_{\text{trans}} &= \delta_{\text{trans}} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_3 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2 + \alpha_4 (\delta_{\text{rot1}}^2 + \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2)) \\ \hat{\delta}_{\text{rot2}} &= \delta_{\text{rot2}} + \mathcal{N}(0, \alpha_1 \delta_{\text{rot2}}^2 + \alpha_2 \delta_{\text{trans}}^2) \end{aligned}$$ The parameters $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$ are *robot-specific* noise gains (in $m^2$ or $\text{rad}^2$) — a frequently-tested KTU question. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ATE Calculation Suppose a robot's true trajectory across **5 time-steps** and the estimator's output are: | $t$ | Ground Truth $x_t^{\text{gt}}$ (m, m, rad) | Estimate $\hat{x}_t$ (m, m, rad) | |---|---|---| | 1 | (0.00, 0.00, 0.00) | (0.10, -0.05, 0.02) | | 2 | (1.00, 0.00, 0.00) | (1.15, 0.08, -0.01) | | 3 | (2.00, 0.00, 0.00) | (1.92, -0.10, 0.03) | | 4 | (3.00, 0.00, 0.00) | (3.20, 0.05, -0.02) | | 5 | (4.00, 0.00, 0.00) | (3.85, -0.07, 0.04) | Compute the Absolute Trajectory Error. The squared translational error at each step is: $$\begin{aligned} e_1^2 &= (0.10)^2 + (-0.05)^2 = 0.0100 + 0.0025 = 0.0125 \\ e_2^2 &= (0.15)^2 + (0.08)^2 = 0.0225 + 0.0064 = 0.0289 \\ e_3^2 &= (-0.08)^2 + (-0.10)^2 = 0.0064 + 0.0100 = 0.0164 \\ e_4^2 &= (0.20)^2 + (0.05)^2 = 0.0400 + 0.0025 = 0.0425 \\ e_5^2 &= (-0.15)^2 + (-0.07)^2 = 0.0225 + 0.0049 = 0.0274 \end{aligned}$$ Sum: $\sum e_t^2 = 0.1277$. Mean: $0.1277 / 5 = 0.02554$. Square root: $$\text{ATE} = \sqrt{0.02554} \approx 0.1598 \text{ m} \approx 16 \text{ cm}$$ This is a *good* localization result for a 2D ground robot (sub-20 cm). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Simulation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid flowchart captures the complete data-flow of a KTU-grade localization simulation project — from world creation to ground-truth benchmarking. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Map Designer
2D floor plan or
occupancy grid] --> B[Virtual Robot
URDF model
+ physics engine] B --> C[Motion Driver
true pose x_t_gt
+ commanded u_t] C --> D[Sensor Simulator
range / camera / IMU
+ injected noise] D --> E[Raw Measurements
z_t] E --> F[Motion Model
p of x_t given u_t x_t-1] F --> G[Bayes Filter
EKF or Grid
or MCL backend] E --> H[Sensor Model
p of z_t given x_t m] H --> G G --> I[Belief State
bel of x_t] I --> J{Convergence
Check N_eff} J -->|N_eff low| K[Resample
M particles] K --> G J -->|N_eff high| L[Pose Estimate
x_hat_t] L --> M[Visualizer
RViz or matplotlib
particle cloud] L --> N[Error Metric
ATE and RPE] C --> N N --> O[Performance Report
convergence plots
and tables] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: MCL Internal Dataflow ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Previous Particles
X_t-1] --> SM[Sample from
Motion Model] SM --> NP[New Predicted
Particles X_bar_t] NP --> LW[Compute Likelihood
w of i = p of z_t given x_bar_t i m] LW --> NW[Normalize Weights
sum equals 1] NW --> EC[Effective Count
N_eff] EC --> RS{Resample
Yes or No} RS -->|Yes| RS2[Low Variance
Resampler] RS2 --> OUT[Output Particles
X_t equal weight] RS -->|No| OUT2[Output Particles
X_t weights intact] OUT --> EST[Best Estimate
x_hat_t] OUT2 --> EST ``` ### 4.3 Simulation Environment Selection Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Choose Simulator] --> Q1{Need 3D
physics} Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Gazebo + ROS
industry standard] Q1 -->|No| R2[Stage or ROS 2D
fast lightweight] R1 --> R3[Camera and LiDAR
plugins available] R2 --> R4[Ideal for KTU lab
projects and exams] Q1 -->|Want Web| R5[Webots by Cyberbotics
browser based] Q1 -->|Want MATLAB| R6[MATLAB Robotics Toolbox
academic friendly] ``` > [!TIP] > **For KTU 2024 lab evaluations**, the *Stage + ROS 1 / ROS 2 Humble* combination is the most likely supported stack because it is open-source, runs on low-cost college PCs, and integrates natively with the AMCL package (`amcl` = Adaptive Monte Carlo Localization). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define Monte Carlo Localization (MCL). Why is it preferred over EKF localization for the *global localization* problem?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Monte Carlo Localization is a sample-based implementation of the Bayes filter that represents the posterior belief $bel(x_t)$ by a set of $M$ weighted particles $\{(x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]})\}_{i=1}^{M}$ drawn from the target distribution. > > **Why preferred (1.5 marks):** EKF localization assumes a *unimodal Gaussian* belief. In the global localization problem, the initial belief must be *uniform* (or multi-modal — many plausible robot locations). A Gaussian cannot represent a uniform distribution, causing the EKF to diverge immediately. MCL, being non-parametric, can represent *any* distribution including multi-modal ones, and converges to a unimodal Gaussian naturally once the robot localizes. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **List the four components of the *beam sensor model* used in 2D LiDAR simulation, and state one physical reason for each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > 1. **$p_{\text{hit}}$** — Gaussian centred on the true range. *Reason:* beams usually return the correct distance. > 2. **$p_{\text{short}}$** — Exponential distribution. *Reason:* unexpected obstacles (people walking, transparent objects) cause early returns. > 3. **$p_{\text{max}}$** — Point mass at maximum range $z_{\max}$. *Reason:* beams that miss all obstacles return the sensor's maximum. > 4. **$p_{\text{rand}}$** — Uniform distribution $[0, z_{\max}]$. *Reason:* phantom / cross-talk / specular reflection noise. > > (Full mark requires the four names, the formula form, and one physical cause each.) --- ### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **Module 2 Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B** --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a robotic localization simulation project. Identify and briefly explain **any four** functional blocks. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the *Monte Carlo Localization (MCL) algorithm* starting from the recursive Bayes filter. Clearly state the role of *importance sampling* and *resampling* in your derivation. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Block Diagram (7 marks):** > A clean labelled block diagram showing: > > 1. **Map Module** — Stores the environment as an occupancy grid or landmark set $m$. (1.5 marks) > 2. **Ground-Truth Simulator** — A physics engine (Gazebo / Stage) that runs a virtual robot and emits the true pose $x_t^{\text{gt}}$. (1.5 marks) > 3. **Sensor Simulator** — Adds noise to true measurements to produce $z_t$. For LiDAR, ray-casts in the engine and adds the four-component beam noise. (1.5 marks) > 4. **Bayes-Filter Backend** — Executes the algorithm (EKF / Grid / MCL) to maintain $bel(x_t)$. (1.5 marks) > 5. **Visualization & Evaluation** — Renders belief and computes ATE/RPE. (1 mark) > > **(b) Derivation (7 marks):** > - Start with the Bayes filter integral (1 mark) > - Approximate $bel(x_{t-1})$ as a sum of Diracs (1.5 marks) > - Importance sampling to draw $x_{t-1}^{[i]} \sim bel(x_{t-1})$ (1.5 marks) > - Propagate through motion model $p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ (1 mark) > - Importance weight $w_t^{[i]} = p(z_t \mid \bar{x}_t^{[i]}, m)$ (1 mark) > - Normalize + resample (1 mark) --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the *sampled odometry motion model* for a differential-drive robot. Define the parameters $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$ and the three motion increments $\delta_{\text{rot1}}, \delta_{\text{trans}}, \delta_{\text{rot2}}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The ground-truth trajectory of a mobile robot across **3 time-steps** in a 2D plane is $x^{\text{gt}} = \{(0, 0, 0), (1, 1, \pi/4), (2, 2, \pi/2)\}$. The MCL estimator (with $M=1000$ particles) outputs $\hat{x} = \{(0.05, -0.02, 0.01), (0.92, 1.08, 0.78), (2.10, 1.95, 1.55)\}$. Compute the **Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE)** and comment on whether the localization is acceptable for a warehouse AMR. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(a) Sampled Odometry Model (7 marks):** > - Decompose relative motion into 3 increments (1.5 marks) > - State the three update equations (3 marks) > - Explain $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ (rotational noise) and $\alpha_3, \alpha_4$ (translational noise) (1.5 marks) > - Justify Gaussian noise assumption (1 mark) > > **(b) ATE Computation (7 marks):** > > $$\begin{aligned} > e_1^2 &= (0.05)^2 + (-0.02)^2 = 0.0025 + 0.0004 = 0.0029 \\ > e_2^2 &= (0.92 - 1)^2 + (1.08 - 1)^2 = 0.0064 + 0.0064 = 0.0128 \\ > e_3^2 &= (2.10 - 2)^2 + (1.95 - 2)^2 = 0.0100 + 0.0025 = 0.0125 > \end{aligned}$$ > > Sum = $0.0029 + 0.0128 + 0.0125 = 0.0282$ > Mean = $0.0282 / 3 = 0.0094$ > $$\text{ATE} = \sqrt{0.0094} \approx 0.0970 \text{ m} \approx 9.7 \text{ cm}$$ > > **Comment (1 mark):** For a typical warehouse AMR operating in a 1–2 m aisle, an ATE of **~10 cm** is *acceptable but tight* — the system is at the edge of safety margins. Threshold ≤ 5 cm is preferred for high-density warehouses. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the normalizer $\eta$** in the Bayes filter — **-1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing the beam model components** — writing $p_{\text{max}}$ as exponential instead of a Dirac point mass — **-1 mark**. > 3. **Not normalizing particle weights** before resampling — entire resampling step is considered invalid — **-2 marks**. > 4. **Using $x_t^{\text{gt}}$ inside the estimator** (ground-truth leakage) — considered a *fatal logic error* — **-3 marks**. > 5. **Reporting ATE without units** or mixing degrees/radians — **-1 mark**. > 6. **Omitting the kidnapped-robot recovery discussion** in MCL viva — **-2 marks** if asked. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Localization is Bayes filter in disguise** — every algorithm (EKF, Grid, MCL) is just a different *approximation* to the same recursive posterior $bel(x_t)$. - **MCL = sample + weight + resample** — this 3-word mantra is the most common viva question. - **Beam model has 4 components** — hit (Gaussian), short (exponential), max (point mass), rand (uniform) — and the sum of $\alpha$'s equals 1. - **Odometry noise has 4 parameters** — $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$ — they are *robot-specific* and must be tuned empirically. - **ATE is in metres, RPE is in metres-per-step** — never interchange them; never omit the unit. - **Effective particle count $N_{\text{eff}}$** — resample when $N_{\text{eff}} < 0.5 M$ to prevent particle deprivation. - **Kidnapped robot problem** — solved by injecting *random particles* in MCL (Augmented MCL). - **Simulator choice matters** — Stage for 2D KTU labs, Gazebo for 3D industry, MATLAB for academic quick-prototyping. - **Ground truth must NEVER leak into the estimator** — always keep $x_t^{\text{gt}}$ strictly outside the algorithm's input stream. - **Convergence threshold** for a passing KTU project: ATE ≤ 0.3 m translational, ≤ 5° rotational. - **Two pillars of any simulation project** — *fidelity of noise models* and *rigorous ATE/RPE benchmarking* — these are how examiners differentiate an A-grade project from a B-grade one.

topics.title: Projects Focused on Sensor Fusion and Decision Making
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT862 - PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Projects Focused on Sensor Fusion and Decision Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Projects Focused on Sensor Fusion and Decision Making

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Fusion** is the computational process of intelligently combining data from multiple sensors such that the resulting information has **less uncertainty** than would be possible when these sensors were used individually. In probabilistic robotics, sensor fusion is implemented through **Bayesian estimation** techniques (Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, Information Filter) to maintain a posterior belief over robot state variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Making under Uncertainty** in robotics refers to the process by which an autonomous agent selects an optimal action $a^*$ from a set of admissible actions $A$, given an uncertain belief $bel(x_t)$ over the world state $x_t$. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this using **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)**, **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**, and **Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a noisy room and must determine your exact position. You have three sources of information:
1. A **noisy GPS reading** (±5 m accuracy)
2. A **step counter / odometer** (cumulative error, no absolute reference)
3. A **compass** (orientation, but not position)

No single sensor gives you a confident answer. However, by **intelligently combining** all three — weighting each by its known reliability — you can pinpoint your location far more accurately than any one source. This is **sensor fusion**.

Now suppose you also have to decide: *"Should I turn left, right, or stop?"* Each action has a different probability of success based on your (uncertain) belief about the world. **Decision making under uncertainty** is exactly this — choosing the action that **maximizes expected utility** given your belief.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Probabilistic Robotics course treats sensor fusion and decision making as **two sides of the same coin** — fusion builds the belief, and decision making acts on it.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Process noise covariance** $Q$ — typically **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$** in real-world SLAM
- **Measurement noise covariance** $R$ — typically **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{0}$** depending on sensor
- **Number of particles** $N$ in a particle filter — empirically **50–5000** depending on state dimensionality
- **Mahalanobis distance threshold** for outlier rejection — typically **$\chi^2$ with 95% confidence**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Sensor Fusion + Decision Project

Every KTU 2024-aligned project in this module follows a **three-layer architecture**:

| Layer | Function | Algorithms Used |
|-------|----------|-----------------|
| **Prediction Layer** | Propagates state using control inputs $u_t$ | Motion model $p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1})$ |
| **Update Layer** | Corrects belief using sensor measurements $z_t$ | Bayes filter, KF, EKF, PF |
| **Decision Layer** | Selects optimal action $a^*$ from belief | MAP, MLE, POMDP policy |

> [!NOTE]
> The **predict-update-decide** cycle is the heartbeat of every probabilistic robotics project in this module.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\overline{bel}(x_t) = \int p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | Bayes filter prediction step | probability density |
| 2 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \vert x_t) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$ | Bayes filter measurement update | probability density |
| 3 | $\hat{x}_t^- = A\hat{x}_{t-1} + Bu_t$ | KF / EKF state prediction (mean) | m, rad, m/s, etc. |
| 4 | $P_t^- = AP_{t-1}A^T + Q$ | KF / EKF covariance prediction | varies by state |
| 5 | $K_t = P_t^- H^T (H P_t^- H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain computation | dimensionless gain matrix |
| 6 | $\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t(z_t - H\hat{x}_t^-)$ | KF / EKF state update | m, rad, m/s, etc. |
| 7 | $P_t = (I - K_t H) P_t^-$ | KF / EKF covariance update | varies by state |
| 8 | $w_t^{[i]} \propto p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]})$ | Particle filter importance weight | normalized weight |
| 9 | $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (w_t^{[i]})^2}$ | Effective number of particles | count |
| 10 | $a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \sum_{x} U(x,a) \, bel(x)$ | Maximum Expected Utility (MEU) | utility units |
| 11 | $x_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_{x} \, bel(x)$ | Maximum A Posteriori estimate | state units |
| 12 | $d_M(x, \mu) = \sqrt{(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)}$ | Mahalanobis distance for outlier rejection | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Hazard:** In KTU exam answers, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never the raw `|` character — inside LaTeX expressions to avoid breaking table parsers in digital valuation systems.

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Tesla, Waymo):** Fuse LiDAR + Camera + Radar + GPS+IMU using EKF/UKF.
- **Drone Navigation (PX4, ArduPilot):** Fuse IMU + barometer + GPS using complementary or Kalman filters.
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** Fuse wheel odometry + 2D LiDAR scans using particle filters for localization.
- **Underwater ROVs:** Fuse DVL + IMU + USBL using EKF for navigation where GPS is unavailable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) Update for a 2D Range-Bearing Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **highly recurring** in KTU 2024 Probabilistic Robotics exams. Master the linearization step.

### Problem Setup

A mobile robot at state $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ observes a landmark at known position $(l_x, l_y)$. The measurement model is:

$$
z_t = \begin{bmatrix} r_t \\ \phi_t \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2} \\ \text{atan2}(l_y - y, \, l_x - x) - \theta \end{bmatrix} + v_t
$$

where $v_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$ is Gaussian measurement noise. The measurement is **nonlinear**, so we must linearize via the **Jacobian**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Measurement Jacobian $H_t$

The Jacobian of $h(x_t)$ with respect to $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$ is:

$$
H_t = \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_t} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Partial Derivatives of Range $r$

$$
\frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = \frac{-(l_x - x)}{\sqrt{(l_x-x)^2 + (l_y-y)^2}} = \frac{-(l_x - x)}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial r}{\partial y} = \frac{-(l_y - y)}{\sqrt{(l_x-x)^2 + (l_y-y)^2}} = \frac{-(l_y - y)}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial r}{\partial \theta} = 0
$$

> **Logic:** Range $r$ depends only on the relative position $(x, y)$ between the robot and the landmark, not on robot orientation $\theta$.

### Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivatives of Bearing $\phi$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{-(l_y - y)}{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2} = \frac{-(l_y - y)}{r^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = \frac{(l_x - x)}{(l_x - x)^2 + (l_y - y)^2} = \frac{(l_x - x)}{r^2}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta} = -1
$$

> **Logic:** The bearing measurement is the angle **to the landmark relative to the robot's heading**, hence a change in $\theta$ produces an equal and opposite change in $\phi$.

### Step 4 — Assemble the Final Jacobian

$$
H_t = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{-(l_x - x)}{r} & \dfrac{-(l_y - y)}{r} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{-(l_y - y)}{r^2} & \dfrac{(l_x - x)}{r^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the EKF Update Equations

$$
K_t = P_t^- H_t^T (H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R)^{-1}
$$

$$
\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t \left( z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-) \right)
$$

$$
P_t = (I - K_t H_t) P_t^-
$$

This completes the EKF update for a single range-bearing landmark observation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Particle Filter Weight Update & Resampling Threshold

### Step 1 — Importance Sampling Principle

Each particle $x_t^{[i]}$ represents a hypothesis with importance weight:

$$
w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]})
$$

> **Logic:** Particles that better explain the measurement receive higher weights.

### Step 2 — Normalize Weights

$$
\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = \frac{w_t^{[i]}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_t^{[j]}}
$$

This ensures $\sum_i \tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = 1$, producing a valid probability distribution.

### Step 3 — Compute Effective Sample Size

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2}
$$

> **Logic:** When one particle dominates, $N_{\text{eff}} \to 1$; when weights are uniform, $N_{\text{eff}} \to N$.

### Step 4 — Resampling Trigger

If $N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically $N/2$), perform **stochastic universal sampling (SUS)** to resample with replacement proportional to weights.

### Step 5 — Sample Mean Position (Decision Output)

$$
\hat{x}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_t^{[i]} \, x_t^{[i]}
$$

This weighted mean is the **decision-layer output** passed to higher-level planners.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MAP-based Decision Making

The robot must choose action $a^* \in A$ to maximize the expected utility $U(x, a)$:

$$
a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \, \mathbb{E}_{x}[U(x,a)] = \arg\max_{a \in A} \sum_{x} U(x,a) \, bel(x)
$$

> **Logic:** The robot does not know the true state $x$, so it averages utility over all possible states, weighted by current belief $bel(x)$.

### Worked Numerical Example

Let the action set be $A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3\}$ and the belief over 3 discrete states be $bel = [0.5, 0.3, 0.2]$. The utility matrix is:

$$
U = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 & 2 \\ 6 & 8 & 5 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $U(x_i, a_j)$ is the utility of action $a_j$ in state $x_i$. The expected utilities are:

$$
\mathbb{E}[U(a_1)] = 0.5(10) + 0.3(4) + 0.2(2) = 5.0 + 1.2 + 0.4 = 6.6
$$

$$
\mathbb{E}[U(a_2)] = 0.5(6) + 0.3(8) + 0.2(5) = 3.0 + 2.4 + 1.0 = 6.4
$$

$$
\mathbb{E}[U(a_3)] = 0.5(3) + 0.3(6) + 0.2(9) = 1.5 + 1.8 + 1.8 = 5.1
$$

Since $6.6 > 6.4 > 5.1$, the optimal decision is $a^* = a_1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Sensor Fusion + Decision Project Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Hardware Layer] --> B[Sensor Data Acquisition]
    C[Wheel Encoders] --> B
    D[IMU Inertial Unit] --> B
    E[LiDAR Scanner] --> B
    F[Camera RGB] --> B
    B --> G[Preprocessing and Noise Filtering]
    G --> H[Bayes Filter Engine]
    H --> I[Prediction Step Motion Model]
    H --> J[Update Step Measurement Model]
    I --> K[Posterior Belief bel of x]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Decision Layer}
    L --> M[MAP Estimate Selector]
    L --> N[Expected Utility Maximizer]
    M --> O[Action Output a star]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Motor Controller and Actuator]
    P --> A
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Solid arrows show data flow. The closed loop from $P$ back to $A$ shows the perception–action cycle. The decision layer is the **bridge** between probabilistic estimation and physical action.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — EKF-based IMU + GPS Fusion Project

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GPS Receiver Position p gps] --> C[Measurement Model z equals h of x]
    B[IMU Accelerometer and Gyro] --> D[Motion Model x dot equals f of x plus u]
    D --> E[State Prediction x hat minus]
    D --> F[Covariance Prediction P minus]
    C --> G[Linearized Jacobian H t]
    G --> H[Kalman Gain K t]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Innovation v t equals z minus h of x hat minus]
    I --> J[State Update x hat equals x hat minus plus K t times v t]
    J --> K[Corrected Pose Estimate]
    F --> L[Covariance Update P equals I minus K t H P minus]
    L --> K
    K --> M[Decision Layer Action Selection]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Particle Filter Monte Carlo Localization Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize N particles uniformly in map] --> B[For each time step do]
    B --> C[Sample motion model to predict new particles]
    C --> D[Acquire LiDAR scan z t]
    D --> E[Compute importance weight p of z t given x t for each particle]
    E --> F[Normalize weights to sum equals 1]
    F --> G{Is N effective less than N threshold}
    G -->|Yes| H[Perform stochastic universal resampling]
    G -->|No| I[Skip resampling]
    H --> J[Compute weighted mean pose estimate]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Decision Layer pick best action via MEU or MAP]
    K --> L[Send velocity command to robot]
    L --> B
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Decision-Making Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Belief bel of x] --> B[Generate candidate actions A]
    B --> C[For each action a compute expected utility]
    C --> D[U expected of a equals sum over x of U of x a times bel of x]
    D --> E[Select a star equals argmax a of U expected of a]
    E --> F[Execute action a star]
    F --> G[Observe new sensor data z t plus 1]
    G --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define sensor fusion and state any two advantages of fusing multiple sensors over using a single sensor in a robotic system.**

**Model Answer:**

Sensor fusion is the process of combining data from multiple sensors to produce an estimate with **lower uncertainty** than any individual sensor. **Advantages:** (1) **Improved accuracy** — combining sensors reduces random noise through statistical averaging. (2) **Robustness / redundancy** — if one sensor fails, others can maintain operation. *(3-mark complete)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) and Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) in the context of robotic decision making.**

**Model Answer:**

MAP selects the state that maximizes the **posterior** $p(x \vert z)$: $x_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_x \, p(z \vert x) \, p(x)$. MLE selects the state that maximizes only the **likelihood** $p(z \vert x)$: $x_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_x \, p(z \vert x)$. **Difference:** MAP incorporates a prior $p(x)$, while MLE ignores prior knowledge. *(3-mark complete)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** A mobile robot uses an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** to localize itself in a 2D environment. The state vector is $x_t = [x, y, \theta]^T$. The robot receives a range-bearing measurement of a landmark at known position $(l_x, l_y) = (5, 3)$ m. At the current predicted state $\hat{x}_t^- = [2, 1, 30°]^T$, compute the **measurement Jacobian matrix $H_t$**. Assume measurement noise covariance $R = \text{diag}(0.1, 0.05)$ and predicted covariance $P_t^- = \text{diag}(0.2, 0.2, 0.1)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the Jacobian from part (a), compute the **Kalman gain $K_t$** and the **updated state estimate** $\hat{x}_t$ for a measured value $z_t = [3.6 \text{ m}, 20°]^T$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute predicted range $\hat{r}$ and bearing $\hat{\phi}$:**

$$
\hat{r} = \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (3-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\hat{\phi} = \text{atan2}(3-1, 5-2) - 30° = \text{atan2}(2, 3) - 30° = 33.69° - 30° = 3.69°
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian entries:**

$$
H_t = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{-(5-2)}{3.606} & \dfrac{-(3-1)}{3.606} & 0 \\[8pt] \dfrac{-(3-1)}{3.606^2} & \dfrac{(5-2)}{3.606^2} & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.832 & -0.555 & 0 \\ -0.154 & 0.231 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Note units:** Convert $\phi$ to radians for the final filter implementation:
$\hat{\phi} = 3.69° \times \pi/180 \approx 0.0644$ rad.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute innovation $v_t$:**

$$
v_t = z_t - h(\hat{x}_t^-) = \begin{bmatrix} 3.6 - 3.606 \\ 0.349 - 0.0644 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.006 \\ 0.285 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Note:** $20° = 0.349$ rad.

**Step 2 — Compute the innovation covariance $S_t$:**

$$
S_t = H_t P_t^- H_t^T + R
$$

$$
H_t P_t^- = \begin{bmatrix} -0.832(0.2) & -0.555(0.2) & 0 \\ -0.154(0.2) & 0.231(0.2) & -1(0.1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1664 & -0.1110 & 0 \\ -0.0308 & 0.0462 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
H_t P_t^- H_t^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1664 & -0.1110 & 0 \\ -0.0308 & 0.0462 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.832 & -0.154 \\ -0.555 & 0.231 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Computing element-by-element:

- $(1,1)$: $(-0.1664)(-0.832) + (-0.1110)(-0.555) + 0 = 0.1384 + 0.0616 = 0.2000$
- $(1,2)$: $(-0.1664)(-0.154) + (-0.1110)(0.231) + 0 = 0.0256 - 0.0256 = 0.0000$
- $(2,1)$: $(-0.0308)(-0.832) + (0.0462)(-0.555) + (-0.1)(0) = 0.0256 - 0.0256 + 0 = 0.0000$
- $(2,2)$: $(-0.0308)(-0.154) + (0.0462)(0.231) + (-0.1)(-1) = 0.0047 + 0.0107 + 0.1 = 0.1154$

$$
S_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2000 & 0.0000 \\ 0.0000 & 0.1154 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3000 & 0.0000 \\ 0.0000 & 0.1654 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Kalman gain $K_t$:**

$$
K_t = P_t^- H_t^T S_t^{-1}
$$

Since $S_t$ is diagonal:

$$
S_t^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/0.3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/0.1654 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.333 & 0 \\ 0 & 6.045 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -0.832 & -0.154 \\ -0.555 & 0.231 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3.333 & 0 \\ 0 & 6.045 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K_t = \begin{bmatrix} -0.1664 & -0.0308 \\ -0.1110 & 0.0462 \\ 0 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3.333 & 0 \\ 0 & 6.045 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
K_t = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5546 & -0.1862 \\ -0.3700 & 0.2793 \\ 0 & -0.6045 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute updated state $\hat{x}_t$:**

$$
\hat{x}_t = \hat{x}_t^- + K_t v_t = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0.5236 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -0.5546 \\ -0.3700 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}(0.285) + \begin{bmatrix} -0.1862 \\ 0.2793 \\ -0.6045 \end{bmatrix}(0.000)
$$

> **Note:** Converting $30° = 0.5236$ rad.

$$
\hat{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 0.158 \\ 1 - 0.105 \\ 0.5236 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.842 \text{ m} \\ 0.895 \text{ m} \\ 0.5236 \text{ rad} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The updated pose estimate is $\hat{x}_t = [1.842 \text{ m}, 0.895 \text{ m}, 30°]^T$. **(14 marks complete)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Particle Filter algorithm** for robot localization with a clear block diagram. State the importance weight update equation and the resampling condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot uses a **Particle Filter** with $N = 100$ particles to localize itself. After a measurement update, the normalized weights are:
$w^{[1]} = 0.05, w^{[2]} = 0.10, w^{[3]} = 0.60, w^{[4]} = 0.20, w^{[5]} = 0.05$.
Compute the **effective number of particles** $N_{\text{eff}}$ and determine whether resampling is needed (threshold = $N/2 = 50$). **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The **Particle Filter** (also called Sequential Monte Carlo method) approximates the posterior belief $bel(x_t)$ using a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles) $\{x_t^{[i]}, w_t^{[i]}\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

**Algorithm steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Sample $N$ particles $x_0^{[i]} \sim p(x_0)$ from the prior distribution.
2. **For each time step $t$:**
   - **Motion update:** Sample $\hat{x}_t^{[i]} \sim p(x_t \vert u_t, x_{t-1}^{[i]})$ for each particle.
   - **Measurement update:** Compute importance weight: $w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \vert \hat{x}_t^{[i]})$.
   - **Normalization:** $\tilde{w}_t^{[i]} = w_t^{[i]} / \sum_j w_t^{[j]}$.
   - **Resampling condition:** If $N_{\text{eff}} = 1 / \sum_i (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2 < N_{\text{threshold}}$, perform **stochastic universal resampling** to replace low-weight particles with copies of high-weight ones.
3. **Output:** The weighted mean $\hat{x}_t = \sum_i \tilde{w}_t^{[i]} \hat{x}_t^{[i]}$ or the MAP particle.

**Importance weight update equation:**

$$
w_t^{[i]} = w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]})
$$

**Resampling condition:**

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\tilde{w}_t^{[i]})^2} < N_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{N}{2}
$$

> **Block diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.3 above for the Monte Carlo Localization architecture.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the sum of squared normalized weights:**

We are given already-normalized weights $w^{[i]}$ that sum to 1. Verify: $0.05 + 0.10 + 0.60 + 0.20 + 0.05 = 1.00$ ✓

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} (w^{[i]})^2 = (0.05)^2 + (0.10)^2 + (0.60)^2 + (0.20)^2 + (0.05)^2
$$

$$
= 0.0025 + 0.0100 + 0.3600 + 0.0400 + 0.0025 = 0.4150
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $N_{\text{eff}}$:**

$$
N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{0.4150} \approx 2.41
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to threshold:**

$$
N_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50
$$

Since $N_{\text{eff}} = 2.41 < 50$, **resampling is required**. After resampling, particle 3 (weight 0.60) will dominate the population, with approximately 60 of the 100 new particles originating from it. This indicates severe **particle depletion**, justifying immediate resampling. **(14 marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the linearization step** in EKF derivations — students often write the standard KF equations without computing the Jacobian. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Not converting degrees to radians** when mixing trigonometric terms in EKF math. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Misapplying MLE vs. MAP** in decision-making questions — the prior $p(x)$ is the *defining* difference. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting to normalize particle weights** before computing $N_{\text{eff}}$ — unnormalized weights make $N_{\text{eff}}$ meaningless. **(-2 marks)**
> 5. **Confusing expected utility maximization with argmax over states** — students often write $x^* = \arg\max U(x,a)$ instead of $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_x U(x,a) bel(x)$. **(-2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Sensor fusion** combines multiple sensors using **Bayesian estimation** to reduce uncertainty.
- The **Bayes filter** has two steps: **predict** $\overline{bel}(x_t)$ and **update** $bel(x_t)$.
- The **Kalman Filter (KF)** is optimal for linear-Gaussian systems.
- The **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** linearizes nonlinear models via the **Jacobian** $H_t = \partial h / \partial x$.
- The **Kalman gain** $K_t$ balances **process noise** $Q$ and **measurement noise** $R$.
- The **Particle Filter** uses $N$ weighted samples to represent arbitrary (non-Gaussian) beliefs.
- **Importance weights** are proportional to the measurement likelihood $p(z_t \vert x_t^{[i]})$.
- **Resampling** is triggered when **$N_{\text{eff}} < N_{\text{threshold}}$** (typically $N/2$).
- **MAP** uses the posterior $p(x \vert z)$; **MLE** uses only the likelihood $p(z \vert x)$.
- **Maximum Expected Utility (MEU)** is the canonical decision rule under uncertainty: $a^* = \arg\max_a \sum_x U(x,a) \, bel(x)$.
- The **Mahalanobis distance** is used for outlier rejection in sensor measurements.
- A typical KTU project has **three layers**: prediction, update, decision.
- Real-world projects include **EKF-based IMU+GPS fusion**, **particle filter Monte Carlo localization**, and **occupancy grid SLAM with decision making**.
- **Process noise $Q$** is added during prediction; **measurement noise $R$** is added during update.
- The **Jacobian for range-bearing** observation has a **zero in the top-right corner** (range does not depend on orientation).
- **Action selection** is a separate layer from **state estimation** — do not conflate them in KTU answers.
- In KTU exam numericals, **always show intermediate steps** — examiners award partial marks for setup even if the final number is wrong.
- **Particle depletion** is a major weakness of particle filters; mitigate with resampling, injection of random particles, or KLD-sampling.
- The **Information Filter** is the dual of the Kalman Filter, useful for decentralized multi-robot fusion.
- **POMDPs** generalize decision making to the case where the robot must act under partial observability over long horizons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT862 - PROBABILISTIC ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study Analyses and Practical Demonstrations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Case Study Analyses and Practical Demonstrations** in Probabilistic Robotics refers to the systematic retrospective and prospective examination of real-world robotic systems in which Bayesian inference, probabilistic state estimation, and stochastic algorithms are deployed to solve the fundamental problems of *perception*, *localization*, *mapping*, and *SLAM* (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping). Under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, this module falls within the higher cognitive levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Analyze, Evaluate, Create — Levels 4, 5, and 6)**, requiring students to deconstruct published algorithms, evaluate their field performance, and synthesize novel deployment strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PERUT862 / Module 3):** The case study approach is *not* a survey of algorithms. It is the rigorous empirical validation of probabilistic robotics theory using documented deployments in autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs), aerial robots (UAVs), underwater vehicles (AUVs), and space exploration platforms. The standard yardsticks are **accuracy** (meters of localization error), **computational cost** (complexity in $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$), **robustness** (recovery from kidnapping / sensor failure), and **scalability** (map size in landmarks or area in $\text{m}^2$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **detective entering a dark, unfamiliar mansion** with only a flickering flashlight and a notebook. The detective must:

1. **Estimate** where they are in the mansion (localization).
2. **Sketch** the mansion as they walk (mapping).
3. **Update** both estimates every time a new hallway or door is observed (Bayesian update).

A *case study* in probabilistic robotics is essentially the detective's **case file** — a fully documented investigation of a real robot (e.g., NASA's *Mars Rover Perseverance*, *Waymo's self-driving taxi*, *Boston Dynamics Spot*) that faced the same mansion problem. We open the file, study which flashlight (sensor) the robot used, which estimation rule (Bayes / Kalman / Particle filter) it applied, and where it succeeded or failed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English:** A case study is a *post-mortem of a real robot*. It tells you: "Here is the algorithm, here is the environment, here is what happened, and here is what the data says."

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted in **Bold**)

| Metric | Definition | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ATE (Absolute Trajectory Error)** | RMSE of estimated vs. ground-truth pose | $0.01 \text{ m} - 5.0 \text{ m}$ |
| **RPE (Relative Pose Error)** | Drift over fixed time/distance intervals | $< 0.1\% \text{ of path length}$ |
| **Computational Complexity** | Asymptotic order of the algorithm | $\mathcal{O}(N)$, $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$, $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ |
| **Memory Footprint** | RAM used for map storage | MBs to GBs |
| **Particle Count** | For PF / FastSLAM, number of hypotheses | $100 - 10,000$ particles |
| **Convergence Rate** | Iterations to reach posterior consensus | Function of sensor noise |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Probabilistic Robotic Stack (Decomposed)

Every case study in this module evaluates the same logical pipeline. Understanding the pipeline is essential to dissecting any real-world deployment.

- **Step 1 — State Definition:** Identify the random variable $x_t$ representing the robot's pose (position + orientation) at time $t$.
- **Step 2 — Motion Model (Prediction):** Propagate belief using control input $u_t$:
$$p(x_t \mid x_{t-1}, u_t)$$
- **Step 3 — Sensor Model (Measurement Update):** Update belief given measurement $z_t$:
$$p(z_t \mid x_t, m)$$
- **Step 4 — Posterior Fusion (Bayes' Rule):** Combine the two via the recursive Bayesian filter:
$$bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t, m) \, \overline{bel}(x_t)$$
- **Step 5 — Decision / Action:** Sample a control from the posterior or use it for path planning.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for case studies:** When you read a published case study (e.g., *Thrun et al., "GraphSLAM", 2006*), every paragraph maps to one of these five steps. Identifying *which step* the paper focuses on is the secret to writing a high-scoring KTU 14-mark answer.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Essential for End-Semester)

| # | Formula | Name | Application in Case Study |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $bel(x_t) = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t) \int p(x_t \mid u_t, x_{t-1}) \, bel(x_{t-1}) \, dx_{t-1}$ | **Bayes Filter** | Universal recursive estimator backbone |
| 2 | $\mu_t = A_t \mu_{t-1} + B_t u_t$ | **Linear Motion (KF)** | EKF SLAM prediction step |
| 3 | $K_t = \Sigma_t C_t^T (C_t \Sigma_t C_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$ | **Kalman Gain** | EKF SLAM correction step |
| 4 | $w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]})$ | **Particle Weight (Importance)** | FastSLAM weight update |
| 5 | $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$ | **FastSLAM Factorization** | Rao-Blackwellised particle decomposition |
| 6 | $\Omega = \sum_t (H_t^T Q_t^{-1} H_t)$ | **Information Matrix** | GraphSLAM global optimization |
| 7 | $p(x_{t+1} \mid x_t, u_t) \sim \mathcal{N}(g(x_t, u_t), R_t)$ | **Gaussian Motion Model** | EKF / UKF / Monte Carlo localization |
| 8 | $J(x) = \sum_t \Vert z_t - h(x_t) \Vert^2_{Q_t^{-1}} + \sum_t \Vert x_t - g(u_t, x_{t-1}) \Vert^2_{R_t^{-1}}$ | **Non-linear Least Squares** | GraphSLAM / Bundle Adjustment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Note on KTU Valuation:** Examiners will *always* award at least 2 marks in a 14-mark question for correctly stating Bayes' rule. Memorize Formula #1 in symbolic form — it is the single most-asked equation in the entire course.

## 2.3 The Three Archetypal Case Study Classes

Every practical demonstration in this module falls into one of three classes:

- **Class A — Indoor / Structured (e.g., museum guides, warehouse AGVs):** Dominated by 2D laser scan matching. Algorithms of choice: *Particle Filter Localization* and *2D EKF SLAM*.
- **Class B — Outdoor / Semi-structured (e.g., autonomous cars, Mars rovers):** Dominated by multi-sensor fusion (LiDAR + IMU + GPS + camera). Algorithms of choice: *GraphSLAM, Pose-Graph Optimization, FastSLAM 2.0*.
- **Class C — Aerial / 3D / Dynamic (e.g., quadrotors, AUVs):** Dominated by visual-inertial odometry (VIO). Algorithms of choice: *MSCKF, OKVIS, ORB-SLAM*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The case study method is the **gold standard** for technology transfer in robotics because it forces students to confront:

- **The reality gap** between simulation (Gazebo, CARLA) and field deployment.
- **Computational constraints** (edge GPU vs. cloud server, embedded ARM vs. desktop x86).
- **Sensor degradation** (rain on LiDAR, dust on cameras, IMU bias drift).
- **Real-time deadlines** (autonomous cars must plan in $< 100 \text{ ms}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The FastSLAM Posterior Factorization

FastSLAM, developed by **Montemerlo, Thrun, Koller, and Wegbreit (2002)**, is the canonical case study of "probabilistic robotics in action." We will derive it step by step. The objective is to estimate the robot's trajectory $x_{1:t}$ and the map $m$ given all sensor data and controls.

### Derivation

We start from the joint posterior:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$$

This joint distribution is intractable for large maps because the state space grows exponentially. FastSLAM uses the **Rao-Blackwellisation** trick — a clever factorization using the chain rule of probability.

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of conditional probability:**

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1}) = p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$$

**Reasoning (textual):** The chain rule states that $P(A, B \mid C) = P(B \mid A, C) \cdot P(A \mid C)$. Here, $A = x_{1:t}$, $B = m$, and $C = (z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$.

**Step 2 — Interpret the first factor $p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$:**

This is the **map posterior given the trajectory**. It can be computed in *closed form* if the landmark positions are conditionally independent given the trajectory. The map of $N$ landmarks factorizes as:

$$p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) = \prod_{n=1}^{N} p(m_n \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t})$$

**Reasoning (textual):** If landmarks are independent (a common assumption in FastSLAM 1.0), then knowing the robot's trajectory makes each landmark's estimate an independent 2×2 EKF. The map is just a *product* of low-dimensional EKFs.

**Step 3 — Interpret the second factor $p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1})$:**

This is the **trajectory posterior** given observations and controls. It is computed via a **particle filter**, where each particle represents a hypothesized trajectory.

**Step 4 — Combine the two factors using importance sampling:**

For particle $i$ with weight $w_t^{[i]}$, the full joint posterior is approximated as:

$$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t-1}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{M} w_t^{[i]} \, \delta(x_{1:t} - x_{1:t}^{[i]}) \cdot p(m \mid x_{1:t}^{[i]}, z_{1:t})$$

**Reasoning (textual):** The Dirac delta $\delta(\cdot)$ picks out the trajectory of particle $i$. Each particle carries a *copy* of the landmark EKFs. The weight $w_t^{[i]}$ is the importance weight:

$$w_t^{[i]} = \eta \, p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m^{[i-1]})$$

**Step 5 — Computational complexity gain:**

Without the factorization, the joint state is a $3 + 2N$ dimensional random variable (3 for pose, 2 per landmark). With the factorization, we have $M$ trajectories (each 3D) plus $M \times N$ independent 2D landmark EKFs. The total cost is $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot N)$ instead of $\mathcal{O}(M^{3+2N})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** FastSLAM's $\mathcal{O}(M \log K)$ for landmark lookup (using a KD-tree, with $K$ as the number of landmarks) is what made real-time SLAM feasible on the 2002-era hardware. This is *the* case study to memorize for KTU.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: EKF SLAM Measurement Update

EKF SLAM is the historical precursor. It maintains a single Gaussian over the full state vector $y_t = (x_t, m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_N)^T$.

**Step 1 — State vector at time $t$:**

$$y_t = \begin{pmatrix} x_t \\ m_1 \\ m_2 \\ \vdots \\ m_N \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{3+2N}$$

**Step 2 — Predicted state using the motion model:**

$$\bar{\mu}_t = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t) = \mu_{t-1} \oplus u_t$$

**Reasoning (textual):** $\oplus$ denotes the pose composition operator in SE(2) or SE(3).

**Step 3 — Predicted covariance:**

$$\bar{\Sigma}_t = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Kalman gain for landmark $n$ observed at time $t$:**

$$K_t^{(n)} = \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{(n)T} \left( H_t^{(n)} \bar{\Sigma}_t H_t^{(n)T} + Q_t \right)^{-1}$$

**Reasoning (textual):** $H_t^{(n)}$ is the Jacobian of the observation model with respect to the full state vector. It is mostly zero, except in the columns corresponding to the robot pose and landmark $n$.

**Step 5 — Update step for landmark $n$:**

$$\mu_t = \bar{\mu}_t + K_t^{(n)} \left( z_t - h(\bar{\mu}_t^{(n)}) \right)$$

$$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t^{(n)} H_t^{(n)}) \bar{\Sigma}_t$$

**Reasoning (textual):** This is the standard EKF update; the cross-covariance terms in $\Sigma_t$ are what *couple* the robot pose to all landmark estimates — a hallmark of SLAM.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Flagship Case Studies

| Dimension | **EKF SLAM (Smith & Cheeseman, 1986)** | **FastSLAM (Montemerlo et al., 2002)** | **GraphSLAM (Thrun & Montemerlo, 2006)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Representation** | Single Gaussian | Particle filter + per-particle EKFs | Sparse graph of soft constraints |
| **Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ per step | $\mathcal{O}(M \log N)$ per step | $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ global; $\mathcal{O}(N)$ incremental |
| **Map Size Scalability** | Poor beyond $\sim 100$ landmarks | Excellent (tested to $10^5$ landmarks) | Excellent (tested to $10^7$ landmarks) |
| **Recovery from Failure** | None (Gaussian dies) | Excellent (multi-hypothesis) | Moderate (depends on initial guess) |
| **Data Association** | Greedy nearest-neighbor | Per-particle ML | Joint compat. test / EM |
| **Real-World Deployment** | Early NavLab, museum robots | Carnegie Mellon mine mapping | Google Cartographer, Kaarta |
| **Computational Cost** | $N^2$ (matrix inversion) | $M \times N$ Kalman updates | Sparse Cholesky factorization |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Probabilistic Robotics Case Study Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    A[Real-World Deployment] --> B[Data Collection]

    H -- Yes --> I[Publication / Deployment]
    H -- No --> J[Tune Hyperparameters]
    J --> D
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure Interpretation:** The loop on the right (`Metrics Satisfied? → Tune → Modeling`) is the **iterative refinement cycle** of every real case study. No robot works on the first deployment — tuning is a fundamental step, not a bug.
